transcript.transcript: My name is Margo harness, and I am a student historian at the College of Idaho. Today is April 11th 2024, and I am with Idaho State Representative, Julie Yamamoto, using Their Story to conduct an oral history related to her experiences in state government and as a Treasure Valley resident. Julie, this oral history interview will provide time for reflection on your life in the Idaho legislature and as an educator, politician, and treasure valley resident. Through these questions, I hope to discuss your experiences with rurality, education, and social and political change over time. I will begin the interview with questions of a more generic nature and allow these questions to lead into conversations about more specific topics. During the interview, I may adjust the order of some of the questions to allow for a more natural flow topics, and I may ask some unscripted follow-up questions to clarify the answers you give. Before we jump into questions, can I have you state and spell your name? Julie Yamamoto, j u l I e y a m a m o t o. Alright. Wonderful. My first question is, where did you grow up and when did you move to the central treasure valley? So I've been I was born and raised in right where Caldwell area, not more than within a like a fifteen mile radius. I did live for one year in Boise when I attended Boise State University for a year, but the rest of the time, I've always lived here in Canyon County and mostly in in Caldwell, or Nampa. So sixty six years. And how have you seen the Treasure Valley change in the recent past culturally? Yeah, culturally, just uh, quite the shift farther right in terms of politics. Those folks do not like to be called extreme, but when you have absolutely zero exceptions for an abortion, whether it's an ectopic pregnancy, or it just doesn't matter. There if the GOP platform now says zero, none, that is an extreme position. When you decide that you know better than a parent, what should, your child should be able to to get in a library and, you want to start, making sure that libraries could lose their insurance if they don't cull their collection to what you consider to be a sanitized library to me, that's extreme. When you decide that, any money that we have that you consider idle should be put into gold and silver when you have, someone who is, that's their business and that they are building this big repository to be able to do exactly that, and that that person is then, supporting campaigns of those that are pushing said legislation, that's extreme. And you'd might not like to be called extreme, but when you take extreme positions, and you're the only one who knows what's true and right and everybody else is not only wrong, but they're evil and that they deserve bad things to happen to them and that you can, vilify them, dehumanize them and say and do all manner of evil, wish them ill, that's extreme. And it's not the Idaho I grew up in, and I've, it has been to my great distress. And commitment to not allow it to go without a fight. So culturally, I've seen a shift of a lot of people moving in from other states, specifically the Bosie State survey that they just showed us this, that I had them present that to house education a lot from California, from Washington, Oregon, and they are calling themselves refugees that they have left these liberal places to come to this last bastion of of freedom and that we are so stupid as long time, lifelong, Idahoans that they have to save us from ourselves by going into government at every level and taking over and then imposing, you know, I don't use, terminology like white Christian nationalism lightly. But I am seeing this movement, especially from those new coming in, that their politics and their religion have melded into such a place that you can't see the difference. And I know, you know, even I say, do I look at I'm a Christian And I and so does it affect everything that I say and do? Oh, I hope it does, but I hope how it does is that means that what does he say? He says to love the lord your God with all your heart, mind, and soul. And the second is like unto it, to love your neighbor as yourself. And that means that and to do good if you have an enemy to not be hateful, but to do good to them. And when I am seeing everything be just the opposite of that that we can call somebody the enemy because they don't agree with us. We have this purity test and if you don't agree, then you are the enemy and you deserve every evil that could befall you like a millstone tied around your neck and thrown into the deepest ocean using scripture to, to vilify demonize and to, somehow make your cause righteous. This is just it's mind blowing to me and that they can't that people cannot see that the very tyranny that they say that they're trying to save us from, that that's what they're indeed imposing on those of us who've lived here all our lives. So, that culturally is a huge shift. Also, that shift of not valuing public education and wanting, to defund government schools and for public tax dollars to go to private institutions. To me, as a lifelong also, Republican conservative. I can't think of anything less conservative than to take public tax dollars and give them to private institutions that have no, no oversight whatsoever, no expectation of that we, as a legislature, know what's going on, with those schools or how those students are faring, even though in the public schools, we have a plethora of expectations and how they have to show us that they are meeting what we have determined to be success, in the the educational setting. So those are those would be two areas that I can say just cultural wise. The thing that hasn't changed which I think is important to note as well is that I do believe that most people who move here that they truly appreciate and want to protect the environment that that we enjoy, and that they expect themselves and others to be good stewards of our land. But what I've also seen is that the while there's this Concern about agriculture and ag land going under, to development. It's only after I have my development, my house. And then we're supposed to stop everything and then no more. And, so it's just... and I don't think that's unusual to hear, but that is what I see. And these political and cultural changes that you're describing. Do you think they happened gradually? And if so, in what time period do you think these started to happen? Right. I think it has been gradual, and I can go back as far as even, Let's see when Bill Sally was first elected to the to congress and now I can't even tell you. I think that was like in the eighties. But so I've watched it over time that, and I feel like that those of us who are considered now the the traditional Republicans that we try to play nice and continue with the big tent, to the point that it's really sped up, I would say, in the last ten years, and then remarkably in the last five. And, there, you know, we can see evidence of that, in the voting for state officers and where it really changed things is down at the precinct committee level. And now we're seeing a, that shift happening. Kootenai County, now Canyon County, and certainly it's, you know, that that's their intent is to take over all those central committees and then tell everybody they don't even think people should get to vote. For, you know, that they should get to vet and decide who runs. Doesn't sound like a constitutional republic, with a Democratic bend, does it? No. No. It doesn't. So moving on to the question, you're actually answered several of my initial questions. So thank you for that. So you mentioned in the original background form that you wanted to talk about how your hobbies have changed as you age and your financial situation history. Could you expand on that? Sure. Well, when I was younger, very much more physically active, and so loved playing a club, well, It was a community based, volleyball. And then I was on club, softball teams, and we actually were a traveling team and, you know, went to even out of state competitions, and it just was so fun. And, I really enjoyed that. But as you get older, your body starts telling you, I think you're done with that, with that, and you better come up with some other things. And so it has become more I start things and then I don't finish them and think, well, I'm gonna save that for when I'm older, like, started stained glass and haven't touched that glass in, over two years now, bought a ukulele and started on it, and then haven't touched it. And part of that is once we're in the legislature, frankly, you know, it's three three and a half months, so that's really all I do. But I'm an avid reader and that hasn't changed at all. I used to garden flowers mostly, but some vegetables, and, and these days, my husband does all install of that. So my job is just to enjoy them, which I like that. And, do some and some traveling, I have only been to out of the country two times, and one time was in Italy. And I would like to I would like to go see more of the world, I think. So I'm hoping to do that before I, you know, while I still have good help to do it. But I have noticed that you have seasons of life, things change, and Oh, and so I started, I got asked to be part of a book club, and I absolutely I enjoyed that. We spend a lot more time talking about everything, but the book we read, but that's okay. No. That's how all book clubs should be in my opinion. You mentioned your husband. How did you meet him? Yeah. So we were both teachers. And, I have been asked to come play volleyball over at West Canyon Elementary because his, brother taught there at the time, and we literally ran into each other because we were both on the same team. We went both for the ball. I'm gonna say again, I called it, but we just boom, you know, so talk about meeting. We it was a clash and then, so it just went from there and we've been married for thirty two years. And when did you run into him? That would have been in 1990. I think it was. Yeah. Yep. And he is one of those teachers that you hope every every student is so lucky that they get to have someone like him. We can't go anywhere that somebody doesn't come up and say, "oh Mr. Sasaki!" You know, and how much they now love either science or math depending on what they had him for. And, he's just he's one of those, one of those that you just think, oh, he was just gifted. And, when did you decide to become an educator and how did that work out? You know, I knew from when I was little. I was probably four or five years old, and I just think that's, not everybody gets, knows, but I knew that was what I was gonna do. And so all the way through, grade school, I was watching what different teachers did and think I'm gonna do that. I'm not gonna do that. That was effective. That's not so effective. And, then there was a time because I had been married before, got divorced, and, back in those days, it was hard to even get a checkings account. I saw that thing that said it was only fifty years ago that women could get a credit card in their own name. And I thought, yep, I understand that because it wasn't that long ago, I struggled just to, where they didn't even wanna open a checking account for me even though I had been the primary wage earner. So, it was my first teaching job. I made twelve thousand six hundred dollars a year. So, I don't know how the cost of living, how that works out to now, but boy, every time my mom invited me to dinner, you can bet I went because it was slim pickins in those days. And, yeah, so I was a teacher and an administrator for thirty two years. And do you think your experiences with your family shaped choosing education specifically at all? No. Probably not. Because, when my dad heard that I had decided I was gonna major in education. He told my mom to tell me that he would pay for my, psychiatric exams because he he knew that it wasn't and he was on a school board. So, you know, it wasn't that he was anti teacher, but he just knew he tells this story about when in negotiations, he and another teacher who is my favorite English teacher of all time, sat down to do the negotiations. And when he showed him his monthly budget, my dad cried and he and and that so that was his issue is he just thought you know, he felt like I could be, much more financially solid in a different career, but, I do not regret that time and my time at all. And then my sister, older sister went into education as well. And so, there are two of us that that have been, that was our primary vocation. And for me, it was never just a job. It was always my vocation and my avocation. I felt like that I was gifted that amount of time to try to do help, shape lives and help them somebody be their best self. I was always one who believed in the whole idea of self actualization and that my job wasn't to try to fit them into what I thought they should be. My job was to try to help them figure out who they wanted, who they could, and who they wanted to be in the world. And so always asking the, so what now what question, even from when I talk first grade, that it was a it was a heavy responsibility, but it was a it was a blessing. You know, to feel like that you got to work with, and I wasn't perfect, believe me. There are people who I felt like I still feel like I should send them sympathy cards or apology cards because you just you're a person that you make mistakes and I, especially as an administrator. There were times when I wish I could have done some of those things differently. But you do the best you know to do. Yeah. Do any specific memories come to mind? Yeah. There was a there was a time when, we had very strict rule about attendance and then participating in sports. And, I just happen to be walking down the hall, and this is the day of the state track meet. And we're into, I think, second or third period. And in walks this young woman who's part of the track team, and I and I looked at her and I said, please tell me you're not just getting here. Well, she told me the truth. She was just getting there. Though she missed class, so then she couldn't participate. And I called that Idaho High School Activities Association, and they just said, well, it's your call. I mean, it's your thing. And I stuck to I stuck to the policy, and she didn't get to participate. And I think that she has resented me to this very day. And I get it. I mean, I, you know, and I think the younger I was, the more black and white I was, and the older I've that I get, the more I see the world isn't always black and white and shouldn't be either. And it so if I had to do that one over again, I think I would have, done I think I would have let her participate. I hope I would have. I don't know. It just, you know, for the fact that it still is over twenty years ago and it still bothers me, you know, Another thing that bothers me about things is like, we had very strict dress code as well and because most of the time who was not, who was violating the dress code were females. They all got sent to me. To deal with because I was the only female administrator. And so, you know, there there are some young women that are out there today who feel like that, I body shamed them that I was judgmental about, it in a way that was hurtful to them. And, you know, I, I mean, I think I still don't think that you should have things cut so low where you're hind end hanging out and all that good stuff. But gosh, I don't know. Maybe we could have done we could have approached that a little bit better. Those are things that I remember that I just think. And you know, it's so funny because I will tell you the majority of people will say, and I get cards, texts, emails, things on messenger, Facebook things, that are positive that are the good things and the the times that I was helpful, but I think I don't know some personalities and I'm one of those that I tend to, remember the negative stuff more than I do the positive and maybe get too bogged down with it. Yeah. I understand that. But I also think it's important that you need to acknowledge you weren't perfect and that you, you know, where you hurt somebody intentionally or not try. [Inaudible] What you sure should try to not keep doing it. You know? Yeah. Yeah. That makes sense. And you've been an educator and an administrator, how have you seen the Idaho Education System change over time? Right. Well, we we have this funny. The irony is that we often and we when I say we is like the legislature as a as a whole that we spend less per pupil, but we expect more. And we have really been, to use, the previous state board of education president's term, we've been punching above our weight for a long time, but you can't continually not meet the needs of, schools and then expect, the buildings, the teachers, the administrators to to keep operating at this high level, academically. We use test scores in ways that they weren't intended to be used and compare them in ways that aren't, accurate comparisons, and most people don't like statistics, but, they're happy to use them when they feel like they make their point that the failing public schools, which I believe is one of the most absolutely false and dangerous narratives that's happening in Idaho education right now. It's been happening across the United States, so it's not like we're the only ones but, this whole idea that the schools public schools are failing, and that's why we need this free market opportunity of, letting parent, the money follow the child. But I'm just, you know, I'm going to say I looked at my property tax from this year And it's under a thousand dollars that I pay to Caldwell Schools And yet the legislature is, considering through a tax credit that was gonna be first, it started out, like, seven thousand, then I think six or five thousand. But I keep thinking, how in the world does does any conservative Republican think that if I'm paying in a thousand dollars or less, that I should be getting five thousand dollars for every child I have to take them to whatever that that money should follow the child. And if you can figure out how that makes sense, I'd be happy to listen, but I it just doesn't to me. And that's what I've been seeing over time is just this let's keep sending the message out that the schools are doing, Marxist ideologies and, critical race theory and, all the schools have porn in them and or obscene materials and, that we, have to protect kids because parents can't do their jobs. Oh, but wait, we're gonna do a, a parent rights bill that say that they're the most important. Our GOP platform says that that the parents are the the ones who make all these decisions until we decide they don't, which is, gender blocking stuff and, surgeries, and, which I'll admit I voted yes on that one about the the surgeries. I thought, well, I think maybe you ought to wait till you're eighteen or older to make a decision that's gonna be surgically impossible to, change, but some of those other, you know, the other things as far as medical, things are concerned. We, and education, I think we should, if we're gonna say parents should decide, then parents should decide. But, nope. We jump in here and there and decide we know best. And, so that's what I've been seeing. Just a a real lack of — to say we love teachers, but all of our actions and other rhetoric would suggest otherwise. We say we care about education, but we don't give them the money they need. We have, reports that show that buildings are falling down around their, heads literally and figuratively. And that they have raw sewage, backing up underneath their, cafeterias, but we don't want them to be able to [inaudible 0:10] anything other than just the information because, even to say that a school is over crowded, that that's that's an opinion and shouldn't be allowed. So But, you know, it's to me, it looks like a a planned effort. To, diminish the role of public, of free public common education and to, turn things over to, private for profit organizations. When did you when do you recall this push towards privatization of education? When do you recall it first entering the discourse? I think that when I first noticed it was, Jeb Bush in Florida, so that's been several years ago. And there were if you look at it with the eye, you can see it. The rumblings of it well before that. But it's just been an incremental over time this creep to where we are now. And just, there are a lot of for profit education organizations that are pouring a lot of money into this, what, like I said, which I think is a planned effort to [inaudible 0:05] the with schools and to, privatize and, oh, that's their idea of the free market is that these for profit and private institutions, that inculcate values also, but they are apparently the right values, so it's okay. Yeah. And if it's alright, I'd like to ask a few questions about your family. Is that..? Sure. Okay. So could you give me your parents' and siblings' names? My dad at Archie Yamamoto and his his parents came from Japan. And so he was first generation, American, Idahoan, and then my mom is Marlene Clark, and she was born in Oklahoma and then relocated here with her family. And then my brother is Chris. He's the oldest, and then Leanne is two years younger than him, and then I'm in the middle, and then I have a sister, Lorinda, and a, younger sister, she's two years younger. And then a sister, Susan, who's four years. So we're all two years apart. And when you hear your parents' names, what comes to mind? Sorry... My dad passed away in September. And, it has been extremely challenging for me because those two have just been well, they are the foundation of our family and, my my moral compass, I know, is so strong because them, and all the time we spent around a table talking about, you know—and I didn't realize at the time that that's what we were doing, but It was figuring out how do you live your life in such a way that when you're gone, that people will, I mean there are always gonna be a certain number of detractors, but there that people would say, she made the most of the time that she was given, and she truly wanted to leave her community better than because she was there. And, so they have always been, models by doing, not just saying of being active in the community. My dad was, asked by Phil bat to be on the first Idaho Human Rights Commission. My dad completely understood, the Minidoka camp situation and how lucky his family was that we lived in Canyon County and didn't have to be relocated. Just radios taken away any guns, knives that were longer than six inches. So I think and and he was part of, you know, like I said, he was twelve years on the Schoolboard, helped open Valleyview School District, which was a bunch of rural kids that had to pay to go to Nampa, Caldwell, but now have their own school district. And so you know, we've been part of this community and part of growing it into, what it what it is today, and which I still say is a welcoming place that says that the American dream is obtainable. But do we have to do some things that help level the playing field? Yeah. I think we do. And I know equity has a bad, is a bad word to some people, but what does it it doesn't say give people things. It just says, that not everybody has the same opportunity. So how do we allow for someone to be able to also own a home. Also have a career that is meaningful. Also, be able to afford to live life, liberty, and pursuit of happiness. And, so equity isn't, is giving somebody what they they need to be successful. And I think it's, or providing, you know, giving us probably the — I always think this hand up instead of a handout is a good way to look at that. Anyway, back to my parents, all of that philosophy that that was inculcated by watching and hearing and and seeing two lives lived and in such a way that that, they definitely both have given more to the community than they ever took. Yeah. And you mentioned in another interview that I saw you, did you mentioned your grandmother. Could you tell me some memories or thoughts about your grandmother? Yeah. So she, her husband, he had known her from Japan, but they were there was enough of an age difference. So there was there was no, you know, at that time, it would have been kind of weird. But, when he decided he was gonna settle. He sent back for her and she came. And, but grandma Yamamoto, Eiko Yamamoto, was an American through and through. And she studied and studied so that she could take the civics exam. And, was a very proud American. The downside to that is that she would not teach us any Japanese because she said we're Americans and we're gonna do you're gonna be good Americans and we're, you know, I have left. She went back and visited Japan, her family, and when she came back, she said she wasn't this was home now and she wasn't going back. And so it was unfortunate because as she got toward the end of her life, she forgot her English and reverted back to Japanese, and we couldn't converse with her because we, we didn't learn to speak Japanese. I, I agreed with that too. But she was always a hard worker too. And, I remember one time my mom told her to come out—to go go out and tell grandma to quit weeding out there that it's too hot and tell her to come in and and, you know, cool off. So I went out there and she just said, she says, "well, when I stop working, I'll die." So I said, "oh, okay." So I picked up a little hoe and started helping her because I thought, well, don't want her to die. So, there you go. Just that strong work ethic, and if you're gonna be here and you need to be gainfully employed no matter, you know, what it is. And she was just, and she taught me, how to do — She was quite a dancer, and she was also a poet. Now I didn't I couldn't read her poetry because it was in Japanese. And like I said, not only did she not teach us to speak, but we can't write it. But, she taught me a, a fan dance so I could be part of the Obon Festival in Ontario. And it was quite — just loved it. She is a remarkable human being. I spent a lot of time with her and my other grandparents too, and you know, when you have five kids in a family, it's kinda neat to be the one. So, yeah. And you mentioned the Obon Festivals. Can you tell me what those were? And all anything and everything about them? So the Japanese community would, put together this festival, and it has everything, the dancing parade, their food. Oh, really good food. And those Japanese ladies, they knew how to cook. It was just really good. And, and not all raw. I mean, a lot of that sashimi and that sort of thing, yeah, but a lot of this stuff was It was cooked and it was delicious. I loved it. So, and it was just a way to keep the heritage and the culture alive. They kinda quit doing it during, World War II because they did not want to —their allegiance was to the United states —and they didn't want to be doing anything because they used to teach, they used to have Japanese class where they would teach to write and to, speak Japanese because they didn't want their kids to lose that. But all that kind of went away during World War II. So the, JACL, the Japanese American Citizens League, they were part of that too, and they would have other, like, picnics and where kids we'd get together and run races and all that good stuff. And you know, it was interesting too because we're half. So half Japanese from dad's side and and Caucasian from mom's side. I know my mom sometimes felt like, that she wasn't very welcome and wasn't really accepted. And, and we maybe felt that to a lesser degree, but I guess it never — I feel no antagonism or— it's just hard. You know, people tend to put themselves into little boxes and our family was just never content to be in a box. Yeah. Have you ever faced any kind of hostility or backlash because your family refuses to fit into those boxes? Well, it's been a — it's been interesting. Even when I was in high school, you know, I always thought I just am a person that on a December seventh, these girls brought raw eggs and they were throwing them at another Japanese gal and me and and saying, you know, you know, "How does it feel jap?" and they're like, they're bombing us with these eggs and stuff? And, I thought that was really weird. After, we went to the pizza — after a football game, and, you won't — you know who cher is I'm sure — but she had a song out that was called Half breed. And so, this gal gets up, and she was one of the egg throwers. And she dedicated that song to me. And I just thought, this is just—this is weird. What it what in the world is you know, why do people have to do these things? But even when I was a principal at Caldwell High, I was talking to these kids that were in the welding shop, and they were doing these white pride things, and I was telling them, you know, we, we don't do that here. Here, we're all Callwell Cougars. We're blue and gold, and we, you know, and the one kiddo just looks right at me and he said, well, you know, you're the absolute worst because you're neither white nor jap you're you're half. You're half. As I read the Harry Potter's and listened to her write about muggles, you know, I was thinking, but I really kinda related to to Hermione and and her being a muggle because I thought, why I don't know what it is about people that they just feel like they have to always box you up and just say, this is where you fit. This is how you fit. And, so yep, I think you know, there are times that people thought I that I and maybe others in my family, we didn't know our place. But you know what our place is is to do whatever good you can do in the world in whatever way you can, and it doesn't matter to me what your your race, your ethnicity, your, you know, your gender, your— just go out and do good good to the world and that, that's just it. Yeah. And did you hear similar stories from your other family members? No. They thought it was really weird that I had all those problems, you know, the only one that even remotely remembered any of that was, the sister that's two years younger, we went to a two room school, and we had moved from one school to another. And we were well accepted at the one school, but when we got to this new—this more country-like school — we were not accepted, and part of it was because of our race, and they were tormenting her one day on the playground and, it ended up in a big circle around, and I, I think I got to thump on about five boys. And in those days I was bigger, you know, I'm 'bout the same — I was probably almost the same height I am now in by fourth grade — And so I was actually about as, about the same or bigger than some of those kids. And, and then I was angry because you just don't you don't put a little kid on a merry-go-round and spin it around so fast that she's a that she's afraid for her life and has crying and out begging them to stop, and they're not. To this day it makes me so mad. And, so I, you know, I feel like, the rest of them have said no. They really didn't, they were surprised to hear those stories because it just didn't it didn't happen to them. Or if it if there was something there, they didn't they didn't feel it the same way I did, I guess. Has your, identity or family history ever affected your political career in any way? Yeah. I think it's partly why when we were talking about some different issues and we would have students come and — the one in particular, she was a transitioned was trying to talk to us and I know that there there are some that they just can't they can't or they won't accept it. But for me, I see a person and I see someone whose heart beats just like mine and who is — has a story to tell and I they I owe it to them, to be respectful, to listen, and to even though it's not my lived experience, it is theirs, and I don't get to deny them their experience and who they, who they are. I need to hear them out, and then I need to do my best to make decisions that are in the best interest of all. And, so I hope it has given me a sense of a little more empathy, you know, a little better sense of we don't know the path someone has taken or the things that they have endured or that they're they're fighting with, and our job is I — I think that when that Steven Covey said seek first to under understand, then be understood, there's a lot to be said for that. Yeah [Inaudible 43:55]. You've decided you've labeled someone and then decided that they're voice isn't worth hearing. Yeah. Yeah. For sure. So either in, I suppose in politics specifically, are there any important memories you've made in this field that come to mind when you think about your political career? I'm gonna tell you the thing that I am most proud of and that is we worked on two pieces of legislation and it truly was a collaborative effort that we had people from the governor's office. We had people from the State Board of Education, the State Department, the Idaho School Board Association, the school administrators, the IEA, Democrats and Republicans more far to the right, more kind of mid. And, one of those, one of them didn't make it, but one of them did. And it was the trailer bill to five twenty one. That's how I believe legislation should be done is that when you're really looking to say, what needs to happen to meet the needs, to best meet the needs of everyone in at least fiscally responsible way, but in a way that people — just because you talk to someone, doesn't mean you get to get up on the floor and say they had they had their, you know, they've been part of this in and kind of give the impression that they're bought into it. Very, disingenuous, and to me, unacceptable. It's when they can point to and say that was a that was a collaborative component of this. Maybe everybody doesn't get everything they want, but but what is there is enough good that it was worth doing. And, that to me is the — my best, moments are always those times. And the other thing I would say is on house education, that committee I was I've been told over and over by one person that he gets the most complaints about the education committee. It's the worst committee, and and I thought, you know, that's funny you would say that because what I get more often is it is the best committee because people are allowed to say what they think and what's on their minds, ask questions, and a lot of them. And that that's when, the best legislation occurs is when people are, you know — there was that one piece of legislation about, not wanting to have gay pride flags and other kinds of flags in in classrooms and, Senator Trekel, who's my senator from district eleven, he was support he was sponsoring that bill. And and he he was kind of aggravated at the time, but in retrospect, he when he and I talked about that, he said, You know, I wish that those questions would have been asked on the senate side because I could have made changes that would have made it, a better bill. And see, that's my point is that instead of him seeing that we were being enemies and trying to keep good legislation from happening, we were asking the right questions, the questions that get to "What's the intended consequence?" and "What is the unintended consequence?" and "How do we mitigate the unintended consequences?". That's how good legislation happens. And and those two bills that you're referring to, especially the first one, could you explain what they were exactly? What they did? So the first one was the the first bill that we passed was that five twenty one. And it I'm gonna do this as succinctly as I can because people's eyes will glaze over if they haven't already, but, it is using, it will get property tax relief in as much as sales tax money will go to schools for bonding and for, maintenance. And so when I was talking earlier about how much of my taxes go to Caldwell School District. It should lessen that amount by however much that sales tax can be. That's all dependent on a average daily attendance. That's how it gets, dispersed out. And, So that's a good thing about five twenty one. The use of sales tax to do that and then to lower property tax. It also did a even a zero point zero five more reduction in income tax, which is another tax savings for people. But what it also did, in a unintentionally, is it messed up schools that were on four day weeks and would mean that they wouldn't get they wouldn't be able to access those funds. Well, that wasn't our intent. So, when the trailer did that — the trailer did something that I didn't that I didn't necessarily feel like was important, but again, when we're collaborative and we're trying to do enough that everybody feels like that they can buy on and that they'll accept what is there was that — the State Board of Education Executive Director will now be, the Governor will select that person and they will be, confirmed by the Senate. And in the past, the Governor selects the state board of education members and they select their executive director. And, I really don't have problem with how they have been doing it, but some people have. So that's, that was a give on, like, my part, for example. And the other thing that that trailer bill did is make sure that the charter schools got their, facilities component because when we did that complete rewrite of the charter school, by law, or rules, we left that out. So again, not intended, but that's what that's, you know, when you get busy doing stuff, that's what — if you don't have everybody involved in it that's when you make those, errors and they have to be they have to be fixed. And the other thing that it did is said that you have to be in person to get those dollars or that it would be this lesser if you're a virtual school. Well, that only makes sense because if the money is to go to facilities and you don't have a building then you shouldn't be getting four hundred dollars. You should be getting—they get half of it. So that's what that one did. So you can see it fixed a lot of, It it fixed a lot of unintended consequences of one bill in a trailer bill. And, I was listening to your your interview on the Ranch podcast, and you mentioned that politicians opposing certain bills are sometimes targeted by the NRA or other people. Have you or somebody you know experienced that? And could you expand on what that means? Yes. Well, for example, I there's a billboard currently, I hate for one, that says a champion for children and families. And then on the other side, there's a digital one that has mine that I paid for, but then it has one from a group that, and I'm not sure which one it is. I don't know if it's the Freedom Foundation, their PAC arm, or if it's the Citizens Alliance, or if it's some other, group that—they're all kinda intertwined. But there, they say that I killed school choice in Idaho, because I, have not been a proponent of educational savings accounts, vouchers, or the tax credit. Now bear in mind, the tax credit didn't come through education. They put it through state affairs, probably because they did know it wasn't gonna pass through the edu— House Ed. It would have passed on the Senate Ed side, but it wouldn't pass on our side. So they bypassed us and took it through —no rev and tax, Revenue and Taxation. It couldn't pass out of there either. So then what do they do, they bring it up through ways and means. And they were just bent that they were gonna get that out on the House floor and, and basically what that would do is like I alluded to earlier, put tax dollars to, to families to be able to pay for their kids tuition to a private school or these now for profit educational national companies that fly in here and do whatever they do. And, so yeah, that's it. The billboard. That's not the first time, I had signed a thing from Citizens Alliance, and they didn't like how I voted because they think that if you vote for those appropriation bills, your a tax-and-spend-liberal. And, and, those appropriation bills are gone over by twenty people, ten on the House, ten in the Senate, and they really are trying to make sure that we're not, overspending. And we have put forth a balanced budget every year because it is constitutionally mandated. And, so this year they divided them out all this to say, the, you know, if you're if you vote— Idaho is growing in population. So it costs more for these state agencies and departments to do what constitutionally we are bound to do, but they want to say that, you know, like one that they wanted, they changed it by a hundred dollars and brought it back through. And, because they they wanna be able to not sell that state street transportation building, even though it's already been gone through a process, and we're gonna renege on the deal. Governor Little went ahead and let it go into law, but he's concerned about it and says it's gonna be problematic for Idaho's reputation as far as people wanting to do business with us. See these, these are the kinds of things and so you get targeted because then you're, so stuff is going out through the mail, my mom got one, and she was so aggravated about it. I'm trying to remember what that one— I think I have it. Oh, yeah. Stop voting like a radical liberal, killed education choice, kept the gay pride flag in school classroom, kept obscene materials in school libraries. So I'm just gonna say, well, I really didn't get much of a choice to kill that education a choice, but if I would have voted it, it would have been no. So, you know, is that killing choice? No. I'm on the board of to at Thomas Jefferson Charter School. That's a choice. It's a public school, but it's still a choice. So, you know, and the gay pride flag we talked about and the library bill goof grief We've been bickering over that for three years now. And, you know, finally, the one gets up and says, well, if you don't like that one, there's a worse one coming can go back to the one where we're gonna put them in put librarians in jail. So when did these bills—okay. Well, Let me skip back to a question I was going to ask. You mentioned that the Freedom Foundation is paying for what for these. So I suppose I have a two pronged question. What is the Freedom Foundation that as far as you have experienced interactions with it, and when did it appear? Oh, well, they've been, a friend of mine, Ralph Smade, who has since has passed, he's, who actually gave Wayne Hoffman that start for the Freedom Foundation. And at that time, it was supposed to be a libertarian think tank. And so they were supposed to not set policy, but they they would give information hopefully so that legislators and people would have a better idea of what we're voting for and that sort of thing. Over time, I feel like that, and libertarians are, you know, tend to think less government is is better, and they were very focused on fiscal responsibility, which I can appreciate. And so when I was first there, I would get the spreadsheet too that would say, here are the bills, and here is how that we, [inaudible 56:55] them, and they have certain number of people who, they trust to look at these bills and determine if they're a plus or minus or neutral and almost always if it's spending money, it's gonna be a negative number, and it can go anywhere from a negative one to I don't know. I think that I've seen a negative five before, but I couldn't tell you which one that was. But over time, I feel like that they have I mean, there are legislators who feel like that they're bullies, you know, and I think, well, you're only a bully if I'm letting you change what I do. If I vote how I think it's right, they can be as mad as they want, but, you know, it's still my vote. And so the Idaho Freedom Foundation has—they're Idaho Freedom Action. That is a different thing than the Idaho Freedom Foundation. And so the Freedom Foundation sees themselves as the educational component and the Freedom Action is the PAC component, and they're the ones who, send out that— that do the campaigning and negative ads on people or positive depending on, their support. Citizens Alliance have a lot of the same some similar people involved and, same kind of a thing. But, you know, I I was given an opportunity by Citizens Alliance to speak to my votes. And, afterwards, I had a call from from a fellow who was, supportive of them. And he said, you know, you, you actually did a really good job of showing how your votes are actually they are conservative, and they all they do fit the Republican platform. Now the Republican platform is starting is changing and it's becoming much more specific about how they want people to vote. So like this time when I, I'm having to name several, exceptions about what I am not able to—the preamble I can agree to in its entirety, but there are a lot of exceptions to the actual platform, planks because it's becoming more and more specific about how they want you to vote. And I don't think that's their business. I, you know, I'm supposed to vote to, for all of District eleven and all of Idaho. Not a as, and they call themselves a a private club. Okay. Well, you're a private club and I get that. But I'm also held accountable to all of district eleven voters, not just, not just the GOP, not just the Freedom Foundation, not just Citizens Alliance, not just young Americans for Liberty who are out of state. And that's the other thing. I've seen a lot of out of state dollars come in to, to, change to get things that they want, and they do it by all this negativity and, trying to get somebody to run now even for precinct committeemen, they just say, I just don't feel like that I'm up to that or and my I don't think my family deserves that kinda vitriol and and, negativity. So your family has been affected by this? Yes. Like I have one sister who, I aggravate her. I mean, there's times that she said, you and your ilk. You know, because, I am I feel like I'm as a conservative or a Republican as anybody needs to be, right? And so I really don't buy into their "I'm not too, too conservative or I'm not a Republican", I think, well, if you look at my votes, I think you can see. But I'm a reasonable person, and I'm a pragmatic person, and I'm a person who thinks you should follow the Constitution all the time, not just when it suits your purpose, which is why this school choice thing, with the tax credit or the, you know, public dollars to private entities. Just blowing my mind because it says, right in our Constitution, "a system of free common public schools," I don't know how you get around— How do you get around that? That is the Constitution for Idaho. And so, you know, Yeah. So I have one sister who, and others are are probably closer philosophically, politically to where I am, a brother who I would say is where I am are a little even farther to the right of me. And, you know, but The fact that it gets between families and friends— I the the whole political thing— that's unfortunate. And we are coming up over an hour now. Oh, goodness. We better be done. I have, one last question. Okay. Don't feel pressured to answer it fast if you have a long answer. Is there anything else that I've not asked you that you feel is important to add at this time? I think I alluded to it, but I think as far as for me as a, an Idahoan, Canyon County Caldwell citizen. This idea that we would use our faith in a way that would be weaponized against others, especially vulnerable people, I find unconscionable. And as a, I have been a follower of Jesus Christ since I was sixteen years old. I started off really black and white. I think I alluded to that earlier, but over time— if we are wanting if we truly are wanting to bring heaven to earth, if we truly are wanting for people to understand and know a God who would love you so much that he would send a son to to to take your sin debt on. We don't draw people to the kingdom by being unloving. We don't draw people to the kingdom by pointing our bony judgmental finger at people and telling them that they're gonna go to hell because their sin is worse than my sin because I've developed a hierarchy of sins and yours are worse than mine. And I can see yours, and you can't see mine. And then we're going to do this whole moral we're going to turn our government into, I because I have said to some of them, you know, maybe we should just pass a law that says everybody needs to accept Jesus Christ as their lord and savior and fine 'em two hundred and fifty dollars a pop every time they do something we don't think is Christ-like. Well, how ludicrous, I know, and that's what I'm saying. How ludicrous? He calls us to love. He calls us to care. He calls us when, when Lord was I hungry and you I fed you, when was I naked and you clothed me, When did I take care of the orphans and the widows? And when did I visit you in prison? Well, when you've done it to the least of these, you've done it to me. And so to for me, my, my journey, if I am going to have my faith inform my practice, including at the state house, it has to start from there. And we say that that those domes are where noble ideas—we're a beacon for noble ideas. Well, the noble idea should be that everybody, liberty and justice for all, not for some, not for when I feel like when I decide, life liberty and the pursuit of happiness for all. And, that to me is, caring about others, whether they think like you do or not, and showing that care, I'm gonna go back to your cup of sugar. Let's just give them give people what they need a cup of sugar. And for heaven's sake, give them a cup of sugar. Yeah. I suppose I should end the interview, but I do have one follow-up question. Sure. I apologize. If you'd rather not, I can No. You're good. I just I'm trying to be respectful of your time too. You have a life. Go, ask away. I I have dedicated a significant portion of my life. To preparing for in conducting this interview. So no worries. So this kind of Christian white nationalism that you mentioned. You've said that it wasn't prevalent when you were younger. Can you describe when it started to take hold of the religious discourse around here? That, boy, that's a good question. Again, I think if I were able to trace it back now, I would go a lot farther if I were thinking, you know, it seems like that it really started being a thing, the first year that I was in the legislature, which would have been in twenty twenty. And I know it was happening before that, but you know what? Because that they speak my language in a lot of ways, I was not, I was not aware of how what was percolating underneath that was starting to, to rise to the top. And it's only been, I guess that's—you know, good grief I'm sixty-six years old and I'm just now getting it figured out. But, you know, I had to live it. I had to feel it, have it done to me. Somebody even said that on Facebook, "well, she's getting what deserves because she was part of that, she was buying into that, and now she's getting now she gets to see how it feels, and don't feel sorry for her." Well, I don't feel sorry for me either, but, you know, you do need to learn, and you need to be aware, and you need to when you see when when it is made obvious and clear to you, then you've got to call it what it is. And that's, you know, I I would say in the last four years, I've I've really seen it up close and in person and just how, vindictive, hateful, and vicious and destructive that it and all under the name of Jesus. Yeah. Well, thank you so much for your time, Julie. I'll stop the recording in just a moment. Okay. Margo, it's been a delight. At that hour went quickly.

lines.line: # Introduction to the Oral History Interview

> The speaker introduces herself as Margo Harness, a student historian conducting an oral history interview with Idaho State Representative Julie Yamamoto. The date is April 11th, 2024, and the purpose of the interview is to reflect on Julie's experiences in state government and as a resident of the Treasure Valley. The speaker outlines the interview process, including the nature of the questions and the possibility of unscripted follow-ups for clarification.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Oral History Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces herself as Margo Harness, a student historian conducting an oral history interview with Idaho State Representative Julie Yamamoto. The date is April 11th, 2024, and the purpose of the interview is to reflect on Julie's experiences in state government and as a resident of the Treasure Valley. The speaker outlines the interview process, including the nature of the questions and the possibility of unscripted follow-ups for clarification.

lines.line: > The speaker reflects on the gradual nature of political and cultural changes in Idaho, noting a significant acceleration in the past decade. She traces the beginnings of these shifts back to the 1980s and discusses the impact on state and local politics, including the takeover of central committees by more extreme elements.

metadata.title: Reflection on Gradual Political Changes
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the gradual nature of political and cultural changes in Idaho, noting a significant acceleration in the past decade. She traces the beginnings of these shifts back to the 1980s and discusses the impact on state and local politics, including the takeover of central committees by more extreme elements.

lines.line: > The speaker was born and raised in the Caldwell-Nampa area of Idaho and has lived there for most of her life, except for a year spent in Boise during her university studies. She describes the Treasure Valley as having undergone significant cultural and political changes, particularly a shift towards more conservative, extreme positions on issues such as abortion, library content, and the use of gold and silver for state funds.

metadata.title: Personal and Regional Background
metadata.synopsis: The speaker was born and raised in the Caldwell-Nampa area of Idaho and has lived there for most of her life, except for a year spent in Boise during her university studies. She describes the Treasure Valley as having undergone significant cultural and political changes, particularly a shift towards more conservative, extreme positions on issues such as abortion, library content, and the use of gold and silver for state funds.

lines.line: > The speaker observes a cultural shift towards the political right in the Treasure Valley, with new residents from other states bringing more extreme political views. She discusses the impact of these newcomers on local politics and their efforts to influence government at all levels, expressing concern over the melding of politics and religion into what she describes as white Christian nationalism.

metadata.title: Observations on Cultural Shifts
metadata.synopsis: The speaker observes a cultural shift towards the political right in the Treasure Valley, with new residents from other states bringing more extreme political views. She discusses the impact of these newcomers on local politics and their efforts to influence government at all levels, expressing concern over the melding of politics and religion into what she describes as white Christian nationalism.

lines.line: # Evolution of Hobbies and Personal Interests

> The speaker discusses how her hobbies and interests have evolved with age and changing physical capabilities, transitioning from active sports to more sedentary activities like reading and participating in a book club. She also shares the story of how she met her husband through their shared profession as educators and reflects on her lifelong passion for teaching.

metadata.title: Evolution of Hobbies and Personal Interests
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses how her hobbies and interests have evolved with age and changing physical capabilities, transitioning from active sports to more sedentary activities like reading and participating in a book club. She also shares the story of how she met her husband through their shared profession as educators and reflects on her lifelong passion for teaching.

lines.line: > The speaker describes the political adversity she has faced, including being targeted by political action committees for her voting record and stances on education. She discusses the negative campaigning tactics used against her and the impact on her family, emphasizing her commitment to conservative values and constitutional principles.

metadata.title: Facing Political Adversity and Hostility
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes the political adversity she has faced, including being targeted by political action committees for her voting record and stances on education. She discusses the negative campaigning tactics used against her and the impact on her family, emphasizing her commitment to conservative values and constitutional principles.

lines.line: > The speaker shares her family background, including her Japanese-American heritage and the influence of her parents on her moral compass and community involvement. She speaks fondly of her grandmother's impact on her life, teaching her cultural dances and poetry, and the importance of hard work and American identity in her family's ethos.

metadata.title: Family Influence and Heritage
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares her family background, including her Japanese-American heritage and the influence of her parents on her moral compass and community involvement. She speaks fondly of her grandmother's impact on her life, teaching her cultural dances and poetry, and the importance of hard work and American identity in her family's ethos.

lines.line: > The speaker recounts the emotional impact of her father's passing and the challenges of discrimination she faced due to her mixed heritage. She shares specific incidents from her youth and as a school administrator, reflecting on the hurtful actions of others and her own responses to them.

metadata.title: Personal Experiences with Discrimination
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts the emotional impact of her father's passing and the challenges of discrimination she faced due to her mixed heritage. She shares specific incidents from her youth and as a school administrator, reflecting on the hurtful actions of others and her own responses to them.

lines.line: # Concerns Over Religious and Political Extremism

> The speaker expresses concern over the rise of religious and political extremism, particularly the use of faith as a weapon against vulnerable individuals. She emphasizes the importance of love, empathy, and respect in her faith and political actions, and the need to uphold the principles of liberty and justice for all.

metadata.title: Concerns Over Religious and Political Extremism
metadata.synopsis: The speaker expresses concern over the rise of religious and political extremism, particularly the use of faith as a weapon against vulnerable individuals. She emphasizes the importance of love, empathy, and respect in her faith and political actions, and the need to uphold the principles of liberty and justice for all.

children.text: # Introduction to the Oral History Interview

> The speaker introduces herself as Margo Harness, a student historian conducting an oral history interview with Idaho State Representative Julie Yamamoto. The date is April 11th, 2024, and the purpose of the interview is to reflect on Julie's experiences in state government and as a resident of the Treasure Valley. The speaker outlines the interview process, including the nature of the questions and the possibility of unscripted follow-ups for clarification.

> The speaker reflects on the gradual nature of political and cultural changes in Idaho, noting a significant acceleration in the past decade. She traces the beginnings of these shifts back to the 1980s and discusses the impact on state and local politics, including the takeover of central committees by more extreme elements.

> The speaker was born and raised in the Caldwell-Nampa area of Idaho and has lived there for most of her life, except for a year spent in Boise during her university studies. She describes the Treasure Valley as having undergone significant cultural and political changes, particularly a shift towards more conservative, extreme positions on issues such as abortion, library content, and the use of gold and silver for state funds.

> The speaker observes a cultural shift towards the political right in the Treasure Valley, with new residents from other states bringing more extreme political views. She discusses the impact of these newcomers on local politics and their efforts to influence government at all levels, expressing concern over the melding of politics and religion into what she describes as white Christian nationalism.
# Evolution of Hobbies and Personal Interests

> The speaker discusses how her hobbies and interests have evolved with age and changing physical capabilities, transitioning from active sports to more sedentary activities like reading and participating in a book club. She also shares the story of how she met her husband through their shared profession as educators and reflects on her lifelong passion for teaching.

> The speaker describes the political adversity she has faced, including being targeted by political action committees for her voting record and stances on education. She discusses the negative campaigning tactics used against her and the impact on her family, emphasizing her commitment to conservative values and constitutional principles.

> The speaker shares her family background, including her Japanese-American heritage and the influence of her parents on her moral compass and community involvement. She speaks fondly of her grandmother's impact on her life, teaching her cultural dances and poetry, and the importance of hard work and American identity in her family's ethos.

> The speaker recounts the emotional impact of her father's passing and the challenges of discrimination she faced due to her mixed heritage. She shares specific incidents from her youth and as a school administrator, reflecting on the hurtful actions of others and her own responses to them.
# Concerns Over Religious and Political Extremism

> The speaker expresses concern over the rise of religious and political extremism, particularly the use of faith as a weapon against vulnerable individuals. She emphasizes the importance of love, empathy, and respect in her faith and political actions, and the need to uphold the principles of liberty and justice for all.

story.description: Julie Yamamoto oral history, conducted for the Idaho State Archives in collaboration with The College of Idaho.
story.title: Yamamoto_Julie_OH2824 Hist305
